questions.question: Which local anesthetic was the first to be used clinically?
questions.explanation: Cocaine was the first local anesthetic used by Carl Koller for anesthetizing the cornea in 1884.

questions.question: Which is the shortest-acting local anesthetic?
questions.explanation: Chloroprocaine has the shortest duration of action (15–25 minutes).

questions.question: The sequence of nerve fiber blockade by local anesthetics is:
questions.explanation: Small myelinated A fibers are most sensitive, followed by B and then C fibers. The traditional notion that thin fibers are most sensitive does not hold true.

questions.question: Maximum systemic absorption of local anesthetic occurs after which block?
questions.explanation: Maximum systemic absorption has been seen after intercostal nerve block due to the high vascularity of the intercostal area.

questions.question: Bupivacaine is absolutely contraindicated for:
questions.explanation: Due to high cardiotoxicity, bupivacaine is absolutely contraindicated for Bier's block. Tourniquet failure can release the drug systemically causing fatal cardiac arrest.

questions.question: The drug of choice for bupivacaine-induced ventricular tachycardia is:
questions.explanation: Amiodarone is now considered the drug of choice for bupivacaine-induced ventricular tachycardia. Bretylium is no longer recommended.

questions.question: Which local anesthetic has the safest profile due to significant extrahepatic metabolism?
questions.explanation: Prilocaine has significant extrahepatic metabolism in kidneys, lungs, and by amidase, making it the safest local anesthetic.

questions.question: EMLA cream is a eutectic mixture of:
questions.explanation: EMLA (Eutectic Mixture of Local Anesthetics) is a combination of 5% prilocaine and 5% lignocaine.

questions.question: Which nerve is usually spared during an interscalene brachial plexus block?
questions.explanation: The ulnar nerve (C8-T1) is usually spared with the interscalene approach, making it unsuitable for hand surgeries.

questions.question: The most bothersome complication of supraclavicular brachial plexus block is:
questions.explanation: Pneumothorax is the most bothersome complication of the supraclavicular approach (incidence 1–6%) due to proximity of the pleural dome.

questions.question: Which nerve is usually spared in the axillary approach to brachial plexus block?
questions.explanation: Musculocutaneous and intercostobrachial nerves are spared with the axillary approach and must be blocked separately.

questions.question: Cervical plexus block is best utilized for:
questions.explanation: Cervical plexus block is best utilized for carotid endarterectomy as the patient's awake state is the best monitor to assess neurologic status.

questions.question: Spinal cord in adults terminates at the lower border of:
questions.explanation: Spinal cord extends from medulla oblongata to lower border of L1 in adults and L3 in infants.

questions.question: The sequence of blockade in central neuraxial blocks is:
questions.explanation: In spinal nerve, autonomic fibers are on the most outer side, so the sequence of blockade is autonomic > sensory > motor.

questions.question: The most common complication of spinal anesthesia is:
questions.explanation: Hypotension is the most common complication of spinal anesthesia due to sympathetic block causing venodilatation and decreased venous return.

questions.question: The most common postoperative complication of central neuraxial blocks is:
questions.explanation: Due to significant decrease in postspinal headache, urinary retention has now become the most common postoperative complication.

questions.question: The most preferred vasopressor for spinal hypotension is:
questions.explanation: Ephedrine is the most preferred vasopressor as it increases cardiac output and heart rate by its beta-adrenergic properties.

questions.question: The drug of choice for treatment of post-spinal shivering is:
questions.explanation: Pethidine is the drug of choice for treatment of shivering, followed by tramadol.

questions.question: The most commonly involved cranial nerve after spinal anesthesia is:
questions.explanation: The 6th cranial nerve is most commonly involved (90%) due to its longest intracranial course.

questions.question: The most commonly used needle for epidural anesthesia is:
questions.explanation: Tuohy needle has a blunt bevel with 15-30 degree curve (Huber tip) and is most commonly used for epidural.

questions.question: The most important factor affecting the spread of epidural block is:
questions.explanation: Volume of drug is the most important factor affecting the level of spread in epidural anesthesia.

questions.question: Effort tolerance greater than how many METs is generally associated with lesser complications in cardiac patients?
questions.explanation: Effort tolerance > 4 METs is generally associated with lesser cardiac complications perioperatively.

questions.question: The induction agent of choice for cardiac patients is:
questions.explanation: Etomidate is the induction agent of choice for cardiac patients due to its cardiovascular stability.

questions.question: The muscle relaxant of choice for cardiac patients is:
questions.explanation: Vecuronium is the most cardiac-stable muscle relaxant and therefore is the muscle relaxant of choice for cardiac patients.

questions.question: In right-to-left cardiac shunts, the induction agent of choice is:
questions.explanation: Ketamine increases systemic vascular resistance, which is beneficial in right-to-left shunts to decrease the shunt fraction.

questions.question: A transplanted heart will NOT respond to which drug for bradycardia?
questions.explanation: Transplanted heart is denervated, so bradycardia will not respond to atropine. Direct β-agonists like isoproterenol should be used.

questions.question: Elective surgery should be delayed if diastolic blood pressure is greater than:
questions.explanation: The general consensus is to delay elective surgery if diastolic blood pressure is >110 mm Hg.

questions.question: The inhalational agent of choice for asthma patients is:
questions.explanation: Sevoflurane provides excellent bronchodilation and is the inhalational agent of choice for asthma patients.

questions.question: Intraoperative bronchospasm should first be treated by:
questions.explanation: Intraoperative bronchospasm should first be treated by increasing the depth of anesthesia with inhalational agent (preferably sevoflurane).

questions.question: Thoracic epidural is the most preferred approach for analgesia after which type of surgery?
questions.explanation: Thoracic epidural is the most preferred, most safe, most effective approach for analgesia in thoracic and upper abdominal surgeries.

questions.question: If elective surgery is to be deferred in a patient with URTI, it should be deferred for:
questions.explanation: Airway remains hyperactive for 5-6 weeks after a significant URTI, so surgery should be deferred for 6 weeks.

questions.question: The induction agent of choice for epileptic patients is:
questions.explanation: Thiopentone has anticonvulsant property and is the induction agent of choice for epileptics.

questions.question: After a cerebrovascular accident, elective surgery should be deferred for:
questions.explanation: The odds of serious complications are stabilized at 3 times after 9 months post-stroke.

questions.question: Which type of anesthesia can precipitate multiple sclerosis?
questions.explanation: Spinal anesthesia (but not epidural) and stress can precipitate multiple sclerosis, probably because of direct exposure of local anesthetic to sheathless spinal cord.

questions.question: The induction agent of choice for electroconvulsive therapy (ECT) is:
questions.explanation: Methohexital is epileptogenic, making it the induction agent of choice for ECT.

questions.question: The gold standard scoring system for assessing severity of hepatic disease is:
questions.explanation: Child-Pugh classification is the gold standard scoring system to assess severity of hepatic disease and risk assessment.

questions.question: The induction agent of choice for hepatic patients is:
questions.explanation: Propofol due to its short half-life and extrahepatic metabolism is the induction agent of choice for hepatic patients.

questions.question: The inhalational agent of choice for hepatic patients is:
questions.explanation: Sevoflurane maintains hepatic blood flow best and does not produce trifluoroacetic acid, making it the agent of choice.

questions.question: The muscle relaxants of choice for hepatic patients are:
questions.explanation: Atracurium and Cis-atracurium are metabolized by Hoffmann degradation and are independent of hepatic function.

questions.question: The inhalational agent of choice for renal patients is:
questions.explanation: Desflurane is the agent of choice because it does not produce fluoride. Sevoflurane produces high fluoride levels approaching the renal threshold.

questions.question: In TURP syndrome, irrigating fluid causes:
questions.explanation: TURP syndrome is characterized by fluid overloading, dilutional hyponatremia, and hypo-osmolality from absorption of glycine irrigation fluid.

questions.question: The target plasma sugar level during perioperative period in diabetic patients is:
questions.explanation: The target should be to keep plasma sugar level between 150 and 180 mg/dL during the perioperative period.

questions.question: The induction agent of choice for hyperthyroid patients is:
questions.explanation: Thiopentone possesses antithyroid property and is the induction agent of choice for hyperthyroid patients.

questions.question: Which inhalational agent is absolutely contraindicated in pheochromocytoma?
questions.explanation: Halothane is absolutely contraindicated in pheochromocytoma because it sensitizes the myocardium to catecholamines.

questions.question: Etomidate is contraindicated in which endocrine condition?
questions.explanation: Etomidate causes adrenocortical suppression and is therefore contraindicated in adrenal insufficiency.

questions.question: In myasthenia gravis, patients are resistant to which type of muscle relaxants?
questions.explanation: Myasthenic patients are resistant to depolarizers (requiring higher doses) and hypersensitive to non-depolarizers.

questions.question: The most preferred inhalational agent for myasthenia gravis patients is:
questions.explanation: Desflurane produces maximum muscle relaxation among currently used agents, making it the most preferred for myasthenia gravis.

questions.question: Succinylcholine is absolutely contraindicated in which neuromuscular disease?
questions.explanation: Succinylcholine is absolutely contraindicated in muscular dystrophies as it may produce cardiac arrest due to hyperkalemia and rhabdomyolysis and can precipitate malignant hyperthermia.

questions.question: The most important concern in rheumatoid arthritis patients for anesthesia is:
questions.explanation: Difficult airway due to atlantoaxial instability, cervical spine stiffness, and TMJ arthritis is the most important concern.

questions.question: In ankylosing spondylitis, what can make central neuraxial blocks impossible?
questions.explanation: Fusion of lumbar spine in ankylosing spondylitis may make central neuraxial blocks impossible.

questions.question: The minimum hemoglobin generally accepted for elective surgery in a normal patient is:
questions.explanation: A minimum hemoglobin of 8 g/dL is considered optimal to accept the patient for elective surgery without comorbidities.

questions.question: The most important perioperative goal in sickle cell patients is to avoid:
questions.explanation: The most important goal is to avoid factors that precipitate sickling: hypoxia, acidosis, dehydration, hypothermia, stress, and pain.

questions.question: Barbiturates are absolutely contraindicated in:
questions.explanation: Barbiturates induce aminolevulinic acid synthetase and are absolutely contraindicated in all porphyrias except porphyria cutanea tarda and erythropoietic protoporphyrias.

questions.question: Hyperventilation during neurosurgery should maintain pCO2 between:
questions.explanation: Moderate hypocapnia (pCO2 25-30 mm Hg) is beneficial; excessive hypocapnia (<25 mm Hg) can cause cerebral ischemia.

questions.question: The most rapid method of reducing intracranial tension is:
questions.explanation: Hyperventilation is the most rapid method of reducing ICT by causing hypocapnia and cerebral vasoconstriction.

questions.question: The inhalational agent of choice for neurosurgery is:
questions.explanation: Sevoflurane is preferred due to more favorable effects on cerebral hemodynamics, better preservation of autoregulation, and smoother recovery.

questions.question: The most sensitive tool to detect venous air embolism is:
questions.explanation: TEE is the most sensitive tool, capable of detecting as low as 0.25 mL of air.

questions.question: Central neuraxial blocks are absolutely contraindicated in:
questions.explanation: Medullary coning can cause death if spinal is given to patients with raised ICT.

questions.question: Minimum alveolar concentration (MAC) decreases by what percentage in pregnancy?
questions.explanation: Progesterone decreases MAC by 25-40%, making pregnant patients more susceptible to anesthetic overdosage.

questions.question: All anesthetic drugs cross the placenta EXCEPT:
questions.explanation: All anesthetic drugs cross the placenta except muscle relaxants (only gallamine has significant transfer) and glycopyrrolate.

questions.question: To prevent supine hypotension syndrome in pregnancy, the patient should be:
questions.explanation: A 15° left lateral tilt or wedge under right buttock prevents IVC compression by the gravid uterus.

questions.question: The vasopressor preferred in current practice for obstetric hypotension is:
questions.explanation: Recent trials show phenylephrine produces less fetal acidosis than ephedrine, making it the preferred vasopressor in current practice.

questions.question: When should painless labor be started?

options.D: Only in active phase of labor

questions.explanation: Current recommendation is to start painless labor when the patient demands, irrespective of the stage of labor.

questions.question: In pediatric patients, the narrowest part of the larynx is:
questions.explanation: In children up to 6 years, the subglottis at the level of cricoid is the narrowest part.

questions.question: The most common adverse cardiac event during perioperative period in pediatric patients is:
questions.explanation: Bradycardia is the most common adverse cardiac event in pediatric patients and must be avoided.

questions.question: The inhalational agent of choice for induction in children is:
questions.explanation: Sevoflurane (7-8%) provides smooth and rapid induction and is the inhalational agent of choice for pediatric induction.

questions.question: Which agents are NOT suitable for maintenance in children due to emergence agitation?
questions.explanation: Sevoflurane (20%) and halothane (15%) cause significant emergence agitation, making them unsuitable for maintenance. Desflurane or isoflurane are preferred.

questions.question: Bag and mask ventilation is contraindicated in which neonatal surgical emergency?
questions.explanation: Bag and mask ventilation is contraindicated in diaphragmatic hernia (increases bowel distension), TEF (air enters stomach through fistula), and pyloric stenosis (aspiration risk).

questions.question: The most common cardiac finding in geriatric patients is:
questions.explanation: Decreased ventricular compliance leading to diastolic dysfunction is the most common cardiac finding in old age.

questions.question: MAC of inhalational agents decreases by what percentage per decade of age?
questions.explanation: The MAC of inhalational agents decreases by 4-6% per decade of age.

questions.question: The preferred inhalational agent for geriatric patients is:
questions.explanation: Desflurane provides rapid emergence and has least metabolism, making it preferred for geriatric patients.

questions.question: The preferred position for intubation in obese patients is:
questions.explanation: Ramped (ear-to-sternum) position provides a superior view of laryngoscopy in obese patients.

questions.question: The inhalational agent of choice for obese patients is:
questions.explanation: Desflurane has least metabolism and lowest fat/gas coefficient, making it the agent of choice for obese patients.

questions.question: Water-soluble drugs (non-depolarizing muscle relaxants) in obese patients should be dosed on:
questions.explanation: Water-soluble drugs have limited volume of distribution in obese patients and should be given on ideal body weight basis.

questions.question: The most frequently used gas for creating pneumoperitoneum in laparoscopy is:
questions.explanation: CO2 is most frequently used because it is more soluble in blood, allowing rapid elimination in cases of embolism.

questions.question: Intra-abdominal pressure during laparoscopy should be kept between:
questions.explanation: IAP of 12-14 mm Hg is recommended. Pressure >20 mm Hg can significantly impair renal and mesenteric blood flow.

questions.question: CO2 embolism during laparoscopy shows which pattern on capnography?
questions.explanation: In CO2 embolism, there is a biphasic response: initial rise in ETCO2 (direct injection of CO2 into vessels) followed by fall (pulmonary obstruction).

questions.question: Traction on which extraocular muscle most commonly triggers the oculocardiac reflex?
questions.explanation: Traction on the medial rectus during squint surgery most commonly triggers the oculocardiac reflex (bradycardia, AV block, or asystole).

questions.question: Which IV agent is contraindicated in ophthalmic surgery because it increases IOP?
questions.explanation: Ketamine increases IOP and should be avoided in ocular surgeries. All other IV agents reduce IOP.

questions.question: Nitrous oxide should be avoided in retinal detachment surgery if which substance is used?
questions.explanation: N2O can expand SF6 or perfluoropropane bubbles used in retinal surgery, significantly raising IOP.

questions.question: The most important complication of laser surgery in panendoscopy is:
questions.explanation: Fires are the most important complication of laser surgery. Prevention involves low FiO2, avoiding N2O, and laser-resistant ET tubes.

questions.question: Tonsillar patients should be nursed postoperatively in which position?
questions.explanation: Tonsillar position (on one side, preferably left lateral, with head low) prevents aspiration and laryngospasm from blood trickling down the throat.

questions.question: Nitrous oxide is contraindicated in tympanoplasties because:

options.B: It increases middle ear pressure displacing the graft
options.D: It decreases blood supply to the ear

questions.explanation: N2O is 35 times more soluble than nitrogen and can increase middle ear pressure to 375 mm H2O, displacing the graft.

questions.question: The induction agent often selected for trauma patients in shock is:
questions.explanation: Ketamine is often selected due to its sympathetic system stimulating property, though in severe/chronic shock it may still cause hypotension.

questions.question: In burn patients, succinylcholine is contraindicated for how long?
questions.explanation: Presence of extrajunctional receptors contraindicates succinylcholine in burn patients for one year.

questions.question: The triad of fat embolism syndrome is:
questions.explanation: The classic triad of fat embolism syndrome is dyspnea, confusion, and petechial hemorrhages. Petechial rash is pathognomonic.

questions.question: Maximum duration for which a tourniquet can be kept inflated is:
questions.explanation: Pressure in tourniquet should not exceed more than 2 hours for both upper and lower limbs.

questions.question: At high altitude, rotameter readings are:
questions.explanation: Density of gases decreases at high altitudes, so rotameter under-reads the actual flow rate (error may be up to 4 L/min).

questions.question: The inhalational agent of choice for day-care surgery is:
questions.explanation: Desflurane has the lowest blood/gas coefficient and therefore the earliest recovery, making it the agent of choice for day-care surgery.

questions.question: The combination of choice for total intravenous anesthesia (TIVA) is:
questions.explanation: Propofol + Remifentanil is the combination of choice for TIVA due to rapid onset and short duration of both agents.

questions.question: Pain has been designated as which vital sign?
questions.explanation: Pain has been designated as the fifth vital sign.

questions.question: The most preferred route for postoperative analgesia is:
questions.explanation: Epidural route is the most preferred route for postoperative analgesia.

questions.question: The drug of choice for neuropathic pain is:
questions.explanation: Pregabalin is the drug of choice for neuropathic pain, followed by gabapentin. Exception: trigeminal neuralgia responds best to carbamazepine.

questions.question: Stellate ganglion block is performed for which condition?
questions.explanation: For upper limb CRPS, stellate ganglion block is performed. For lower limb dystrophies, lumbar sympathetic chain block is performed.

questions.question: The mode of ventilation considered as mode of choice in current critical care practice is:
questions.explanation: Pressure-regulated volume control (PRVC) offers combined benefits of volume (guaranteed tidal volume) and pressure (reduced barotrauma) ventilation.

questions.question: The most common source of nosocomial infections in ICU is:
questions.explanation: Urinary tract infections account for 35-40% of total ICU infections, followed by pulmonary (25-30%) and bloodstream infections (20%).

questions.question: The simplest and most cost-effective method to decrease ventilator-associated pneumonia is:

options.C: Positioning head of bed at 30 degrees

questions.explanation: Positioning the head of the bed at 30 degrees decreases gastroesophageal reflux and is the simplest, most cost-effective method to decrease VAP.

questions.question: In CPR, the compression to ventilation ratio for adults without advanced airway is:
questions.explanation: Without advanced airway, the ratio is 30:2 irrespective of number of rescuers for adults.

questions.question: The only recommended method for brain protection after cardiac arrest is:
questions.explanation: Targeted temperature management (maintaining core body temperature 32-36°C for 24 hours) is the only recommended method.

questions.question: The most common cause of pulseless electrical activity (PEA) is:
questions.explanation: Hypovolemia (shock) is the most common cause of PEA among the 5H and 5T causes.
